text:
Mapping gene regulatory networks typically requires vast amounts of transcriptomic data to resolve complex interactions. This reliance on large-scale datasets often hinders discoveries in fields where data are inherently scarce, such as rare diseases or conditions affecting clinically inaccessible tissues. In recent years, transfer learning has transformed fields like natural language processing and computer vision by utilizing deep learning models pretrained on massive, general datasets. These models can then be fine-tuned for a wide range of downstream applications using only limited, task-specific data. 

In this study, we present Geneformer, a context-aware, attention-based deep learning model pretrained on a comprehensive corpus of approximately 30 million single-cell transcriptomes. Our framework is designed to enable high-resolution, context-specific predictions in network biology even when data are limited. We demonstrate that during the pretraining phase, Geneformer acquires a fundamental representation of network dynamics, effectively encoding cellular hierarchy and gene-gene relationships within its internal attention weights in an entirely unsupervised manner. This approach provides a robust foundation for identifying critical regulatory drivers and disease mechanisms across diverse biological contexts.
